Sacramento, cA 94280-00oL
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
our File No. I -91-4 3 5
Dear Ms. Sandronsky:
This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the duties and responsibi I it ies of a nember of the state
jo6 i."i"ing 
coordinating CounciJ. under the conf l ict-of- interest
pio"i-i""= 5f tn" politi6al 
Reform Act (the rrasgl1.1 You have not
!io"ia.a us with the nane of the public officiat 
on l,rhose behalf
you seek our advice. Accordingly, we treat your letter as a
ieouest for informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of
Rejulation ]-8329.2
OUESTI ON
AmenberoftheJobTrainingcoordinatingCouncilisalsoan
officer, board nember, and 18-20 percent owner of a company doing
U"=1".== with pubtic entities throughout the state' 
Do the
conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the Act require this publ-ic
;;;i;i;i 
to disquatiry nimself fro:n participating in decisions to
allocate funds to service delivery areas?
1 co.r.rnr.nt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. AII statutory references
aretotheGovernmentcodeunlessotherwiseindicated.commission
iegufations apPear at 2 california code of Regulations. Section
i;60o, et seq'.' AII references to regulations are to Title 2'
oivision oE the california Code of Regulations'
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor.with the
i,**unity provided by an opinion or. fornal written advice'
(section a:rra; Regulation 18329 (c) ' )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 O Sacramento cA 958O4'O8O7 o (916)322'5660


our File No. I-91-4 3 5
Paqe 2
CONCLUSION
The conf l ict-of-j,nterest provisions of the Act require public
officials to disqualify thenselves frorn participating in
governnental decisions if it is reasonably foreseeabl'e that the
iecisions will have a material' financial effect on the official's
econom.ic interests. 
However, urhen the official's 
source of income
is a public agency, the 'rpublic generally" exception may be
applitable thus allowing the official to participate in decisions
affecting the Public agency.
FACTS
As counsel for the Employnent Development Department, you
seek our advice on behaff of a nember of the State Job Training
coordinating Council (the trcouncilri), which irnplements the Job
Training Partnership Act. 
(29 U.s.C.A. sections 1501-1781.)
council members are appointed by the Governor pursuant to the
nrovisions of 29 u.s.c.A. Section 1532, The Purpose of the Job
iraining Partnership Act ("JTPA'I) is to prepare youth and
unskill6d adults for entry into the }abor force and to afford job
training to those econo:nically disadvantaged individuals and other
individrials facing serious barriers to enployment vrho are in
special need of such training to obtain Productive- enployment ' tl
(ig v.S.c,A. Section 1501.) Funds fron the federal government
iupport job training services adrninistered at the 1ocal leve1
throuqh service deliverY areas.
The council advises the Governor about the designation of
service delivery areas. The council's duties also include
advising the Governor and local' entities on job training plans'
Seventy:eight Percent of the funds for the service delivery areas
within a state are allotted each fiscal year according to a
certain formula. (29 u.S.c.A. Section 1602 (a) (1) . ) six percent
of a state's allotment for each fiscal year is used for incentive
grants for service delivery areas which. exceed certain performance
itandards. (2s U.S.c.A. section 1602(b) (3) (B).) 
one of the
functj,ons of the council is to reconnend variations in performance
standards. (29 v. S.c.A. Section 1532 (b) (2) . )
The council sets criteria for the granting of the Title III
discretionary funds for employnent and traininq assistance. for
dislocated workers. A review cornmittee cornprised of council
members, JTPA representatives and representatives fron other state
agencies evaluatls the proposals for funding from service delivery
aieas and makes a reconnendation to the director of the Emplo].nent
Development Department who then nakes the finat decision reqarding
the aliocation-of funds. council nenbers participate in a panel
that ranks requests for funds disbursed to veterans. (29 U's'c'A'
Section f721. )


Our File No. I-91-4 3 5
Page 3
The member of the council on whose behalf you seek our advice
is an officer, board member, and 18-2ot owner of a business (the
rconpanyrr) which provides engineering, architectural and
construltion Inanagement services to public agencies in california.
The company is tocated in concord, california, 
and AIamitos,
calj,for;ia. 
The company leases the real property on which the
business operates. Most of the public agencies doing business
with the company are cities; however, the company's biggest client
is the Departnent of Transportation ("CaI Trans"). The conpany
often contracts wj.th cal Trans to Provide inspectors who monitor
quality control on cal Trans construction projects. 
The company
irequently contracts with cities to provide inspection and
conitruction managenent for public facilities. 
The city of
Anaheim, which is a service delivery area, j.s one of the clients
of the company. The company obtains most of its contracts through
the competitive bidding process. The conPany does not perform any
job training for service delivery areas.
To the extent that the aforementioned council member votes on
the granting of funds to a private industry council v/hich oversees
the job training plan for a service delivery area (20 U.s'C.A.
Sectlon 103), clients of the colnpany within the service delivery
area benefit. 
Hoi,rever, Job Training Partnership Act funds cannot,
by statute, displace funds used for other purposes. . Funds.
piovided under this act may onLy be used for activities 
which are
in addition to those which lroul'd otherwise be available in the
area in the absence of such funds' (29 U.S.c-A- Section 1551(a).)
ANALYS I S
Section 87100 prohibits public officials 
frorn rnak j'ng,
participating in making, or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governnental decision in which they have a
iinancial interest. 
Appointed members of the Job Training
Coordinating Council are public officials. 
(section 82048.)
Section 87103 specifies that an official 
has a financial
interest in a decision, withi'n the rneaning of Section 87100, if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision witl have a material
financial efiect, distinguishable fron the effect on the public
generally, on the official, 
or on a nember of the official's
immediate familyr or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the pubJ.ic
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official, has a direct or indirect interest $rorth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore.
3  An official′ s :limmediate family:: are his or her spouse and
dependent children.  (SeCtiOn 82029。
)


Our Flle No. I-91-435
Page 4
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terns available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or rnore in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
***
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest neans any investnent or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, 
by an agent on behalf of a
puUlic official, 
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, 
the official's 
agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
inairectty, or beneficially a lO-percent interest
or greater.
For purposes of the Act, an interest in real property
includes a 1lasetrold if the fair market value of the interest is
one thousand dollars or more. (Section 82033.)
Governnental Decision
A public official 
makes a governmental decision or
participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public offici"t 
votes on a natter, commits the agency to a course
-of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency. (Regulation 187o0(b).) AdditionaLly, a public
official 
participates in a governnental decision when, acting
within tnl authority of his or her position, the public official:
(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or
(2) Advises or makes recomnendations to the
dec is ion-rnaker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:
(A) Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of
judgnent on the part of the official 
or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision; or


Our File No. I-9■-435
Page 5
(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise of judgment on the
part of the official 
or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decis ion.
Regulation L8700 (c) .
with regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official's 
agency or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official 
is
attempting to use his or her official 
position to influence the
decision if, for the purPose of influencing the decision, the
official 
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any rnember, officer, ernployee or consultant of the
agency. Attenpts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appeaiances or contacts by the official 
on behalf of a business
enlity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1.)
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
economic interests of the mernber of the state Job Training
coordinating council on h,hose behalf you seek our advice will be
naterially affected by a decision, he nust not only disqualify
himself fiorn participating in forrnal decisions of the council
which nay affect such interests, but he must also abstain front
attempting to influence such decisions by cornmunicatinq with other
nernbeis oi the council or with staff regarding the decisions.
Foreseeabi I itv
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial Iikelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision nust be rnore than a nere
possibility; 
however, certainty is not required. 
(Downev cares v.
Downev Connunitv Development Comn. (1987) 196 caI.App'3d 983, 989-
991; witt v. U.e-E-rgE 0977) 70 cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC ops. 198. The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. 
(witt v. Morrow, suDra at 823.)
It is foreseeable that decisions to aLlocate funds to
jurisdictions where the member of the council does business will
have a financial effect on the public agencies which are sources
of income to the public official. 
If this effect is material, as
discussed belolr, disqua I if ication is required unless the "public
generally' exception applies.
However, if does not appear to be reasonably foreseeable that
allocations of funds to the public agencies will have a financial
effect on the colnpany. You have advised us that the conpany


Our File No. I -91-4 3 5
Page 5
obtains most of its contracts for engineering services through the
competitive bidding process. Moreover, the company does not
p"rio.* any job training for service delivery areas.
aaaitionattY, anY funds allocated to service delivery areas
cannot, by statute, displace funds used for other purposes. Funds
provided Lo the service delivery areas.by the council.may only be
used for activities which are in addition to those which would
otherwise be available in the area in the absence of such funds.
Based upon these facts, i-t would appear that any funds
allocated to a service delivery area by the council will not be
used to free other funds to be used for other Purposes-
Therefore, a public agency whj.ch receives funds from the council
wiII not, as a result of the transaction, have increased funds to
devote to construction and engineering services of the type
provided by the colnpany' Accordingly, the company will not be
iffectea by funds allocated to the service delivery areas.
However, ii it is foreseeable that the decision to allocate funds
to a public agency may have a financial effect on the official's
company, it must be determined lrhether the effect on the company
will be material as discussed below.
Materi.a l itv
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's 
econonic interest in a decision is
naterially affected by a decision. If the official's 
econornic
interest -is directlv involved in the decision, Regulation La702'1-
provides the appropriate standard for assessing materiality.
ilorever, when the official's 
econonic interests wiII be affected
indirecilv by a decision, the appropriate standards for deterrnin-
ing :nateriality are those of Regulations La702.2 through La702.6.
wh6n an official's 
interests in real property are indirectlv
invol.ved in a governmental decision, the appropriate standard for
determining materiality is that of Regulation 18702.3. These
regulations are discussed be low.
The initial 
step in assessing the materiality of the
financial effect of a governnental decision requires a
determination as to whether the involvement of the official's
econonic interest is direct or indirect. 
Regulation 18702.1(b)
states that a person or business entity is directly involved in a
decision before an official's 
agency when that person or entity,
either personally or bY an agent:
(1) fnitiates the proceeding in which the
decision wiII be made by fiting an app).ication,
cIaim, appeal, or sinilar reguest or;


Our File NO. I-91-435
Page 7
(2) Is a naned party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official 
or the official's 
agency.
(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceedinq if a decj-sion involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any J.icense, perrnit, or other entitLement to, or
contrict vrith, the subject person or business
entitY.
Decisions of the council t ill 
have a direct effect on public
agencies v.,hich are the recipients of the funds because such
alencies are the subject of the decision before the official's
ui".,"y. 
when a source of incorne to a public official 
is directly
iivofvea in a decision, the appropriate standard for determining
materiality is that of regulation 18702.1 which states in
pertinent part that the effect of a decision is material if:
Any person (inctuding a business entity) rrlhich
has been a source of incone (including gifts) to
the official 
of 5250 or more in the preceding 12
months is directly involved in a decision before
the official's 
agency... -
Regulation L8702.1 (a) (1) .
Accordingly, any decision of the councit to provide funds to
a service delivery area which has been a source of income to the
official 
is deerned to naterially affect the source of income'
Absent application of the ttpubl j-c generallytt exception discussed
below, disqualificatj,on is required'
The companv as an Economic Interest
when a business entity in which a public official 
has an
economic interest is indirectly affected by a governmental
decision, the appropriate standard for assessing nateriality 
is
that of Regulation 1a7o2.2. For all but the largest business
entities, subdivision (g) of Regulation ta702.2 applies which
states that the effect of a decision is material for a business
entity indirectly affected by a decision if:
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or
(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or elirninating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the anount of 52,500 or more; or


Our File NO. I-9■
-435
Page 8
(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of S10,000 or nore.
Accordingly, if the public official 
determines that granting
funds to a particular jurisdiction which is a client of his
business vi11 increase or decrease the revenues, operating
expenses, or value of the assets and Iiabj-Iities of the business
of which he is a part owner in the anounts stated above,
disqua l if ication will be reguired.
Sources of Incone to the comDanv
Additionally, the public official 
holds an ownership interest
in the conpany of 18 to 20 percent. As an 18 to 20 percent owner
of the conpany, 18 to 20 Percent of aII income to the company is
attributed to the public official. 
Accordingly, the public
official 
nay not participate in any decision which will have a
rnaterial f inancial ef f ect on a pC!S..el!- er-€nt-ily which has provided
inconre to the company.of $1,250 or more in the t$relve nonths
preceding a decision.{ 
( E}e.. Advice Letter, No. I-89-457.)
when a persons e/ho has been a source of incorne of S25o or
nore to a public official. in the twelve rnonths preceding the
decision is indirectly involved in a decision before the
official's 
agency, the appropriate standard for determining
materiality is that of Regulation 18702.6 which states that the
effect of a decision is materiaL if:
(a) The decision will affect the individual's
incone, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities 
(other than real
property) by $r,ooo or nore; or
(b) The decision will affect the individual's
real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under section 18702.3 or
section La7 02.4 .
Public Genera I Iv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is rnaterial, disqual i f ication is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public aenerally.
(section 87100.) If the decision does not affect all, rnernbers of
n ($r,25o x .20) = g25s.
5 "Persont, means an individual, proprietorship, firn,
partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, 
company,
corporation, association, connittee, and any other organization or
group of persons acting in concert. (Section 82047.)


Our FiIe No. I -91-4 3 5
Page 9
the publi.c in the same manner, disqua I i f ication may be required
unlels the effect of the decision on the source of inco:ne is the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703.) The "public generallyl comprises a1l persons
wj.ttrj-n the jurisdiction of the public officiaL. 
(In re owen
(7976) 2 FPPC Ops. 81.) Therefore, the 'rpublic genera]ly'r as
applicable to the jurisdiction of a member of the council- are al-l
peisons vrithin the State of California because members of the
council have state-wide jurisdiction.
A decision will affect a source of income to the publ-ic
official 
in the same lnanner as the public Aenera)')-y if it can be
shown that a significant segment of the public will be similarly
affected by the decision. In the matter of In re Ferraro (L978) 4
FPPC ops. 62, 67, the Commission stated that a group that was
large in numbers and heterogeneous in quality constituted a
sigiificant 
segrnent of the public for the purposes of the xpublic
geierally" exception. Applying these principles, the commission
6oncluded that, for purposes of a rent-control ordinance, owners
of three or fer,rer rental units in the city of Los Angeles
constituted a significant segment of the public.
In the rnatter of In re Owen (L976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77, 81, the
Commission concluded that honeowners in the immediate vicinity of
the trcore area" in the city of Davis constituted a signif i.cant
segment of the public. 
A common thread in the Comrnission's
anilysis of this question appears to be the test first articulated
in F6rraro, S!p-B: \,rhether the group under consideration is large
and-EiveEe with its only common bond being the relationship in
the grouping. Accordingly. for the I'public generally" exceptron
to appIy, the numbers affected nust be sufficiently 
large and
sufficiently diverse.
You have stated that the City of Anaheim, which is a service
delivery area, is one of the company's clients. 
The company
designs parks for the city of Anaheim. Any decj.sion to allocate
fundi to- the city of Anaheim is thus potentially disquatifying if
the City has been a source of income of 51,250 to the company in
the 12 ironths preceding the decision. Horrever, the City of
Anaheim has a large and diverse population of approximately
265.000. Any decision to allocate funds to the city will have an
effect on the entire jurisdiction of the c j-ty and wiLl affect a
substantial segnent of the public, which consists of alI persons
in the State of California, in substantially the salne nanner.
Accordingly, we conclude that a population as large and diverse as
that of the city of Anahein constitutes the "public generally. r'
Therefore, the public officiaL need not disqualify himseLf from
participatj.ng in decisions which wiII affect his source of incorne
,h"n =uth source of income is a public entity comprising a
significant segment of the population of the State of California.


Our File No. 
■-9■-435
Page ■0
We trust this letter adequately responds to yOur 
■nquiry.

not hesitate to Call me at (9■

QUESTION: AmenberoftheJobTrainingcoordinatingCouncilisalsoan
officer, board nember, and 18-20 percent owner of a company doing
